Title: Soteria: The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption Author: Dr. Daniel Folarin Publisher: Great Haven Media info@greathaven.org First Published in April 2025 Book Cover Design: Godis Okonofua

About the Book This book is a journey, one that invites you to think deeply, study carefully, 
and
 
ultimately
 
worship.
 
SOTERIA
 
is
 
about
 
salvation,
 
but
 
not
 
just
 
as
 
something
 
that
 
happens
 
to
 
us.
 
It’s
 
about
 
salvation
 
as
 
the
 
eternal
 
plan
 
of
 
God,
 
settled
 
before
 
time
 
began,
 
and
 
revealed
 
fully
 
in
 
Christ.
 I wrote this book with a conviction that the cross wasn’t God’s reaction to 
the
 
fall,
 
it
 
was
 
always
 
part
 
of
 
His
 
eternal
 
purpose.
 
According
 
to
 
Titus
 
1:2
 
and
 
Romans
 
1:2,
 
eternal
 
life
 
was
 
promised
 
before
 
the
 
world
 
began.
 
That
 
means
 
salvation
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
“Plan
 
B.”
 
It’s
 
not
 
God
 
scrambling
 
to
 
fix
 
what
 
went
 
wrong.
 
It’s
 
the
 
unfolding
 
of
 
His
 
love,
 
wisdom,
 
and
 
grace,
 
right
 
on
 
time.
 Now, I don’t believe God caused the fall, but I do believe He was fully 
aware
 
of
 
it
 
and
 
more
 
than
 
able
 
to
 
redeem
 
us
 
from
 
it.
 
That’s
 
how
 
I
 
understand
 
His
 
sovereignty:
 
not
 
as
 
micromanagement,
 
but
 
as
 
the
 
supreme
 
ability
 
to
 
bring
 
about
 
His
 
will
 
despite
 
the
 
failures
 
of
 
men.
 This book explores key themes like the eternal nature of salvation, the 
centrality
 
of
 
the
 
cross,
 
the
 
right
 
foundation
 
for
 
understanding
 
redemption,
 
and
 
how
 
our
 
view
 
of
 
God
 
shapes
 
everything
 
else.
 
While
 
it
 
touches
 
on
 
theological
 
terms
 
like
 
infralapsarianism
 
and
 
election,
 
it
 
stays
 
rooted
 
in
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word
 
and
 
the
 
simplicity
 
of
 
the
 
gospel.
 I pray that as you read, your heart will be stirred, your mind renewed, and 
your
 
love
 
for
 
Jesus
 
grows
 
stronger.
 
This
 
book
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
about
 
doctrine,
 
it’s
 
about
 
devotion.
 
Because
 
to
 
understand
 
salvation
 
is
 
to
 
stand
 
in
 
awe
 
of
 
the
 
God
 
who
 
saved
 
us.

Dedication To all the saints across the earth, 
believers
 
in
 
the
 
risen
 
Christ,
 
partakers
 
of
 
the
 
heavenly
 
calling,
 
and
 
pilgrims
 
of
 
eternal
 
promise.
 
This
 
is
 
for
 
you,
 
whose
 
hearts
 
burn
 
for
 
His

Preface Salvation, or Soteria , has been at the core of my teachings for as long as I 
can
 
remember.
 
Since
 
the
 
inception
 
of
 
the
 
Life
 
Conferences
 
(2018),
 
I
 
have
 
found
 
myself
 
returning
 
to
 
this
 
foundational
 
subject.
 
The
 
reason
 
is
 
simple:
 
the
 
more
 
I
 
teach
 
about
 
salvation,
 
the
 
more
 
I
 
see
 
its
 
depth
 
and
 
significance
 
and
 
the
 
more
 
I
 
understand
 
why
 
it
 
must
 
be
 
taught.
 Salvation is not just the beginning of the Christian journey; it is the 
foundation
 
and
 
framework
 
for
 
everything
 
we
 
believe
 
and
 
practice
 
as
 
followers
 
of
 
Christ.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
the
 
ABC
 
of
 
the
 
faith,
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
A
 
to
 
Z.
 
Every
 
aspect
 
of
 
theology,
 
whether
 
it
 
is
 
Christology
 
(the
 
study
 
of
 
Christ),
 
pneumatology
 
(the
 
study
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit),
 
eschatology
 
(the
 
study
 
of
 
end
 
times),
 
or
 
ecclesiology
 
(the
 
study
 
of
 
the
 
church),
 
finds
 
its
 
grounding
 
in
 
a
 
proper
 
understanding
 
of
 
Soteria
.
 
If
 
we
 
misunderstand
 
salvation,
 
we
 
risk
 
distorting
 
our
 
view
 
of
 
God,
 
His
 
purposes,
 
and
 
His
 
plan
 
for
 
humanity.
 I believe salvation must be taught, re-taught, and understood deeply. Over 
the
 
years,
 
I
 
have
 
encountered
 
many
 
believers
 
with
 
questions
 
about
 
their
 
faith,
 
doubts
 
about
 
their
 
standing
 
with
 
God,
 
and
 
confusion
 
about
 
the
 
work
 
of
 
Christ.
 
More
 
often
 
than
 
not,
 
these
 
questions
 
stem
 
from
 
an
 
incomplete
 
or
 
flawed
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation.
 
When
 
we
 
do
 
not
 
get
 
Soteria
 
right,
 
our
 
theology
 
in
 
other
 
areas
 
unravels.
 
Conversely,
 
when
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation
 
is
 
clear
 
and
 
biblical,
 
it
 
strengthens
 
every
 
other
 
area
 
of
 
our
 
faith.
 The goal of this book is to lay out the doctrine of salvation in a way that is 
thorough,
 
accessible,
 
and
 
rooted
 
in
 
Scripture.
 
I
 
hope
 
that
 
as
 
you
 
read,
 
you
 
will
 
not
 
only
 
gain
 
a
 
clearer
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
but
 
6

also experience the assurance and confidence that comes from knowing 
who
 
you
 
are
 
in
 
Christ
 
and
 
what
 
He
 
has
 
done
 
for
 
you.
 One of the things I have come to appreciate in my journey of teaching 
Soteria
 
is
 
its
 
transformative
 
power.
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
doctrine
 
to
 
be
 
understood,
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
truth
 
that
 
changes
 
lives.
 
The
 
more
 
we
 
understand
 
salvation,
 
the
 
more
 
we
 
see
 
God’s
 
love,
 
justice,
 
and
 
mercy
 
at
 
work
 
in
 
perfect
 
harmony.
 
We
 
come
 
to
 
realize
 
that
 
the
 
cross
 
was
 
not
 
a
 
reaction
 
to
 
human
 
failure
 
but
 
a
 
carefully
 
orchestrated
 
plan
 
of
 
redemption,
 
rooted
 
in
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose
 
and
 
carried
 
out
 
through
 
Christ.
 I believe that every hand this book touches will encounter the truth of 
God’s
 
Word
 
in
 
a
 
profound
 
way.
 
My
 
prayer
 
is
 
that
 
as
 
you
 
engage
 
with
 
these
 
pages,
 
you
 
will
 
be
 
equipped,
 
strengthened,
 
and
 
made
 
perfect
 
and
 
complete
 
in
 
your
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation.
 
This
 
understanding
 
will
 
not
 
only
 
strengthen
 
your
 
relationship
 
with
 
God
 
but
 
also
 
bring
 
clarity
 
to
 
every
 
other
 
aspect
 
of
 
your
 
faith.
 Salvation is the beginning, the middle, and the end of the Christian journey. 
It
 
is
 
the
 
anchor
 
that
 
keeps
 
us
 
grounded
 
and
 
the
 
lens
 
through
 
which
 
we
 
see
 
God,
 
ourselves,
 
and
 
the
 
world.
 
My
 
hope
 
is
 
that
 
this
 
book
 
will
 
serve
 
as
 
a
 
guide
 
to
 
help
 
you
 
navigate
 
this
 
profound
 
truth,
 
ensuring
 
that
 
your
 
theology
 
is
 
sound,
 
your
 
faith
 
is
 
unshakable,
 
and
 
your
 
life
 
reflects
 
the
 
transformative
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
gospel.
 Thank you for joining me on this journey. Let us dive deep into the riches 
of
 
God’s
 
plan
 
of
 
salvation
 
and
 
discover
 
together
 
the
 
beauty
 
and
 
power
 
of
 
Soteria
.
 Dr. Daniel Folarin 
7

Introduction Salvation, known as Soteria in theological terms, lies at the very heart of 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose
 
for
 
humanity.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
simply
 
an
 
event
 
or
 
a
 
doctrine
 
to
 
be
 
understood,
 
but
 
a
 
journey
 
into
 
the
 
depths
 
of
 
divine
 
love,
 
justice,
 
and
 
mercy.
 
At
 
its
 
core,
 
salvation
 
speaks
 
to
 
the
 
very
 
character
 
of
 
God,
 
His
 
unchanging
 
nature,
 
His
 
eternal
 
promises,
 
and
 
His
 
unrelenting
 
desire
 
to
 
bring
 
humanity
 
into
 
eternal
 
fellowship
 
with
 
Him.
 To grasp the fullness of salvation, we must begin at its origin. Too often, 
discussions
 
about
 
salvation
 
focus
 
primarily
 
on
 
the
 
end
 
result,
 
eternal
 
life,
 
the
 
forgiveness
 
of
 
sins,
 
or
 
the
 
promise
 
of
 
heaven.
 
While
 
these
 
outcomes
 
are
 
certainly
 
glorious,
 
they
 
cannot
 
be
 
fully
 
appreciated
 
without
 
understanding
 
the
 
foundation
 
upon
 
which
 
they
 
rest.
 
Just
 
as
 
a
 
majestic
 
structure
 
draws
 
its
 
stability
 
from
 
the
 
foundation
 
beneath
 
it,
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation’s
 
conclusion
 
depends
 
entirely
 
on
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
its
 
beginning.
 The Scriptures consistently point us to this truth. Titus 1:2 declares that 
God
 
“promised
 
eternal
 
life
 
before
 
the
 
world
 
began.”
 
This
 
statement
 
is
 
profound.
 
It
 
tells
 
us
 
that
 
salvation
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
reaction
 
to
 
human
 
sin
 
or
 
a
 
hastily
 
devised
 
plan
 
to
 
fix
 
a
 
broken
 
world.
 
Instead,
 
salvation
 
has
 
always
 
been
 
central
 
to
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose.
 
Before
 
the
 
foundations
 
of
 
the
 
earth
 
were
 
laid,
 
before
 
humanity
 
was
 
formed,
 
and
 
even
 
before
 
the
 
fall,
 
God
 
had
 
already
 
prepared
 
the
 
way
 
for
 
redemption.
 This eternal nature of salvation dispels common misconceptions. Some 
have
 
misunderstood
 
the
 
cross
 
of
 
Christ,
 
viewing
 
it
 
as
 
a
 
response
 
to
 
human
 
rebellion,
 
a
 
tragic
 
necessity
 
rather
 
than
 
a
 
deliberate
 
act
 
of
 
divine
 
love.
 
Worse
 
still,
 
some
 
critics
 
of
 
Christianity
 
have
 
caricatured
 
the
 
crucifixion
 
as
 
8

a form of “divine child abuse,” where an angry God inflicts punishment on 
an
 
innocent
 
Son.
 
Nothing
 
could
 
be
 
further
 
from
 
the
 
truth.
 The sacrifice of Jesus was not an afterthought, nor was it imposed on Him 
unwillingly.
 
Jesus
 
Himself
 
declared,
 
“I
 
lay
 
down
 
my
 
life,
 
that
 
I
 
might
 
take
 
it
 
again.
 
No
 
man
 
taketh
 
it
 
from
 
me,
 
but
 
I
 
lay
 
it
 
down
 
of
 
myself”
 
(John
 
10:17-18,
 
KJV).
 
His
 
willing
 
sacrifice
 
was
 
the
 
ultimate
 
expression
 
of
 
God’s
 
justice
 
and
 
mercy,
 
planned
 
and
 
foreordained
 
before
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
world.
 
The
 
cross
 
is
 
not
 
evidence
 
of
 
God’s
 
wrath
 
but
 
of
 
His
 
love,
 
a
 
love
 
so
 
profound
 
that
 
He
 
would
 
step
 
into
 
humanity’s
 
brokenness
 
to
 
reconcile
 
us
 
to
 
Himself.
 Reflecting on my own journey, I recall a moment when this truth deeply 
impacted
 
me.
 
For
 
years,
 
I
 
had
 
understood
 
the
 
cross
 
primarily
 
as
 
a
 
necessary
 
act
 
in
 
response
 
to
 
sin.
 
But
 
as
 
I
 
began
 
to
 
grasp
 
that
 
the
 
cross
 
was
 
always
 
part
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
plan,
 
it
 
shifted
 
my
 
perspective
 
entirely.
 
I
 
realized
 
that
 
the
 
cross
 
was
 
not
 
merely
 
a
 
reaction
 
to
 
human
 
sin,
 
but
 
the
 
unfolding
 
of
 
a
 
grand,
 
divine
 
narrative,
 
a
 
love
 
story
 
that
 
began
 
before
 
time
 
itself.
 Theologically, this understanding invites us to reflect on the balance 
between
 
God’s
 
sovereignty
 
and
 
human
 
free
 
will.
 
While
 
God’s
 
sovereign
 
plan
 
for
 
redemption
 
was
 
established
 
before
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
world,
 
it
 
is
 
essential
 
to
 
recognize
 
that
 
the
 
fall
 
was
 
not
 
caused
 
by
 
God.
 
Instead,
 
God,
 
in
 
His
 
infinite
 
wisdom,
 
allowed
 
humanity
 
the
 
freedom
 
to
 
choose,
 
knowing
 
that
 
through
 
this
 
freedom,
 
His
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
would
 
unfold.
 This view acknowledges that the integrity of God’s character will not 
override
 
the
 
will
 
of
 
man.
 
The
 
fall
 
of
 
humanity
 
was
 
not
 
a
 
necessary
 
part
 
of
 
9

God’s plan, but in His sovereign wisdom, He permitted it, knowing that He 
would
 
ultimately
 
use
 
it
 
as
 
a
 
stage
 
for
 
displaying
 
His
 
glory
 
through
 
both
 
judgment
 
and
 
mercy.
 
In
 
this
 
light,
 
salvation
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
mere
 
reaction
 
to
 
the
 
fall
 
but
 
the
 
outworking
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose
 
to
 
redeem
 
and
 
restore
 
mankind
 
to
 
Himself.
 This understanding resonates with aspects of the doctrine traditionally 
referred
 
to
 
as
 
infralapsarianism
,
 
but
 
with
 
a
 
necessary
 
distinction.
 
In
 
this
 
view,
 
God’s
 
decree
 
for
 
salvation
 
was
 
made
 
before
 
the
 
fall
 
of
 
humanity,
 
not
 
as
 
a
 
reaction,
 
and
 
certainly
 
not
 
because
 
the
 
fall
 
was
 
causally
 
decreed
 
by
 
God,
 
but
 
because
 
God,
 
in
 
His
 
infinite
 
foreknowledge
 
and
 
sovereign
 
wisdom,
 
knew
 
humanity
 
would
 
fall.
 
Thus,
 
He
 
lovingly
 
and
 
purposefully
 
predetermined
 
the
 
means
 
of
 
salvation
 
through
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
before
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
world
 
(Titus
 
1:2;
 
Romans
 
1:2).
 
The
 
fall,
 
therefore,
 
is
 
not
 
understood
 
as
 
a
 
necessary
 
or
 
orchestrated
 
part
 
of
 
God’s
 
decree,
 
but
 
as
 
a
 
foreseen
 
reality
 
within
 
which
 
God
 
would
 
reveal
 
His
 
glory
 
through
 
redemption.
 
It
 
affirms
 
that
 
God
 
did
 
not
 
cause
 
the
 
fall
 
but
 
had,
 
from
 
eternity,
 
a
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
already
 
in
 
place,
 
anchored
 
in
 
His
 
unchanging
 
love
 
and
 
eternal
 
purpose.
 This book seeks to delve deeply into the doctrine of Soteria , exploring its 
eternal
 
origins,
 
its
 
profound
 
implications,
 
and
 
its
 
transformative
 
power.
 
In
 
the
 
chapters
 
that
 
follow,
 
we
 
will
 
examine:
 ● God’s Eternal Purpose: We will uncover how God’s plan for 
salvation
 
predates
 
time
 
itself,
 
revealing
 
His
 
unchanging
 
nature
 
and
 
eternal
 
love.
 
Salvation
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
“Plan
 
B”
 
but
 
the
 
centerpiece
 
of
 
God’s
 
design
 
for
 
creation.
 
10

● The Necessity of a Correct Foundation: A flawed understanding 
of
 
salvation
 
often
 
leads
 
to
 
a
 
flawed
 
understanding
 
of
 
God.
 
We
 
will
 
explore
 
why
 
it
 
is
 
essential
 
to
 
begin
 
with
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
nature
 
and
 
purpose,
 
rather
 
than
 
human-centered
 
perspectives.
 ● The Centrality of the Cross: The cross of Christ is the apex of 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan.
 
We
 
will
 
dispel
 
misconceptions
 
about
 
the
 
crucifixion
 
and
 
see
 
it
 
for
 
what
 
it
 
truly
 
is
 
the
 
ultimate
 
act
 
of
 
divine
 
love
 
and
 
justice.
 ● infralapsarianism and Salvation: Though it may seem academic, 
the
 
concept
 
of
 
infralapsarianism
 
offers
 
profound
 
insights
 
into
 
the
 
order
 
and
 
purpose
 
of
 
God’s
 
decrees.
 
This
 
section
 
will
 
provide
 
a
 
clear,
 
accessible
 
explanation
 
of
 
this
 
doctrine
 
and
 
its
 
relevance
 
for
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation.
 ● The Transformative Power of Salvation: Salvation is not merely 
about
 
securing
 
a
 
future
 
when
 
heaven
 
happens;
 
it
 
is
 
about
 
entering
 
into
 
the
 
eternal
 
life
 
God
 
intended
 
for
 
us.
 
We
 
will
 
consider
 
how
 
this
 
truth
 
transforms
 
our
 
lives,
 
our
 
worship,
 
and
 
our
 
relationships
 
with
 
God
 
and
 
others.
 At the end of the book, there is a workbook for each of the chapters. This 
workbook
 
is
 
designed
 
to
 
help
 
you
 
further
 
engage
 
with
 
the
 
material
 
and
 
reflect
 
deeply
 
on
 
the
 
teachings.
 
It
 
will
 
guide
 
you
 
through
 
questions,
 
prompts,
 
and
 
exercises
 
that
 
will
 
reinforce
 
the
 
concepts
 
explored
 
in
 
each
 
chapter.
 Additionally, a teacher’s guide is provided, offering structured support for 
those
 
who
 
wish
 
to
 
lead
 
discussions
 
or
 
teach
 
these
 
principles
 
to
 
others.
 
This
 
11

guide will assist in facilitating thoughtful conversation and ensure that the 
key
 
points
 
of
 
each
 
chapter
 
are
 
effectively
 
communicated.
 As we embark on this journey, I invite you to approach these pages with an 
open
 
heart
 
and
 
mind.
 
The
 
doctrine
 
of
 
Soteria
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
an
 
intellectual
 
exercise;
 
it
 
is
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
worship.
 
To
 
study
 
salvation
 
is
 
to
 
behold
 
the
 
wisdom,
 
love,
 
and
 
glory
 
of
 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
to
 
stand
 
in
 
awe
 
of
 
His
 
justice,
 
marvel
 
at
 
His
 
mercy,
 
and
 
rest
 
in
 
His
 
grace.
 My prayer is that this book will deepen your understanding of salvation 
and
 
draw
 
you
 
closer
 
to
 
the
 
God
 
who
 
authored
 
it.
 
May
 
you
 
see,
 
as
 
I
 
have,
 
that
 
the
 
promise
 
of
 
eternal
 
life
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
about
 
what
 
awaits
 
us
 
in
 
the
 
future
 
but
 
about
 
the
 
eternal
 
purposes
 
of
 
a
 
God
 
who
 
loved
 
us
 
before
 
the
 
world
 
began.
 Let us begin this journey together, exploring the eternal, unchanging, and 
glorious
 
plan
 
of
 
God’s
 
salvation.
 
12

CONTENT PART ONE: THE FOUNDATION OF SALVATION Chapter 1: The Weight of Salvation 17 ● Why Soteria is central to Christianity ● Defining Soteria beyond mere deliverance ● The danger of doing Christology exclusively from below (focusing 
only
 
on
 
Jesus’
 
humanity)
 ● The necessity of understanding Jesus’ work as both fully divine and 
fully
 
human
 Chapter 2: The Eternal Purpose of Salvation 25 ● Salvation was not a reaction to sin but God’s eternal intention ● Titus 1:2 - The promise of eternal life before time began ● Hebrews 2 - The “so great salvation” initiated by Christ ● The preordained nature of Jesus’ sacrifice - debunking the idea that 
it
 
was
 
divine
 
child
 
abuse
 Chapter 3: The Virgin Birth and Its Necessity for Salvation 31 ● Why it was necessary for Jesus to be born of a virgin ● How the incarnation safeguards both Jesus’ divinity and humanity ● The role of the Holy Spirit in Jesus’ conception and its 
soteriological
 
significance
 ● Why explaining the incarnation is vital when proclaiming the 
gospel
 PART TWO: CHRIST AND HIS WORK OF SALVATION Chapter 4: The Kingdom and the Cross 37 
13

● The link between Jesus’ preaching of the Kingdom and His death 
on
 
the
 
cross
 ● Jesus as both King and Sacrifice - the tension between the 
Messianic
 
hope
 
and
 
the
 
suffering
 
servant
 ● How the gospel of the Kingdom includes His atoning death and 
resurrection
 ● Psalm 110:1 - The most cited Old Testament text in the New 
Testament
 
and
 
its
 
significance
 
in
 
Christ’s
 
mission
 Chapter 5: The Redemption - Understanding Christ’s Death 42 ● What is the model of redemption that best explains the nature and 
purpose
 
of
 
Jesus’
 
death?
 ● Survey of Redemption theories: ○ Ransom Theory ○ Satisfaction Theory ○ Penal Substitution ○ Christus Victor ● Why Penal Substitution and Christus Victor together provide the 
most
 
comprehensive
 
understanding
 
of
 
the
 
redemption.
 Chapter 6: The Resurrection and the Necessity for Salvation 55 ● Describe the necessity of the resurrection for salvation ● 1 Corinthians 15 - If Christ is not raised, our faith is in vain ● The resurrection as the validation of Christ’s sacrifice ● The role of the resurrection in justification and eternal security PART THREE: THE GOSPEL AND ITS WORKINGS Chapter 7: Saved by the Gospel 66 
14

● Romans 1:16-17 - The gospel as the power of God unto salvation ● The gospel is not just good advice but good news ● Faith as not demanded but imparted through the hearing of the 
gospel
 Chapter 8: Redemptive History - The Plan for the Gospel 72 ● Order of salvation (Ordo Salutis) - How salvation unfolds in time 
and
 
experience
 ● Predestination, calling, justification, sanctification, and 
glorification
 ● Ephesians 1 - God’s predetermined plan to save in Christ Chapter 9: Images of Salvation - The Result of the Gospel 87 ● Various metaphors for salvation in Scripture: ○ Justification (legal) ○ Redemption (slavery) ○ Reconciliation (relational) ○ Regeneration (biological/new birth) ○ Adoption (familial) Chapter 10: The Scope and Security of Salvation 94 ● How wide is salvation? Who is it for? ● The universal offer vs. the particular application ● Can a believer lose salvation? Addressing eternal security and 
assurance
 ● The perseverance of the saints vs. apostasy PART FOUR: SALVATION AND CHRISTOLOGY Chapter 11: The Deity and Humanity of Christ in Salvation 103 
15

● Is it possible to overemphasize the deity of Christ at the expense of 
His
 
humanity?
 ● How a proper Christology balances both natures ● The errors of Adoptionism - Did Jesus become the Son at baptism, 
or
 
was
 
He
 
always
 
divine?
 ● Why understanding Christ’s dual nature is essential to salvation Chapter 12: The Everlasting Covenant and Salvation 109 ● Isaiah 59:21 - The covenant of the Spirit and the Word ● Isaiah 60:19-22 - The perpetual nature of salvation and God’s 
promises

Chapter 10: The Scope and Security of Salvation Salvation, as revealed in the Scriptures, is a profound and 
all-encompassing
 
reality
 
that
 
transcends
 
human
 
understanding.
 
It
 
extends
 
beyond
 
mere

of

a
 
comprehensive
 
restoration
 
of

believer’s
 
relationship
 
with
 
God,
 
involving
 
every
 
facet
 
of
 
the
 
believer’s
 
existence.
 
In
 
this
 
chapter,

explore
 
the

and
 
security
 
of

from

theological
 
and
 
devotional
 
perspective,

as:
 
How
 
wide
 
is

And

of

 1. The Scope of Salvation: How Wide Is Salvation? The scope of salvation is vast, and the Scriptures present a salvation that is 
both
 
universal
 
in
 
its
 
offer
 
and
 
particular
 
in

application.
 
On
 
one
 
hand,
 
salvation
 
is
 
offered
 
to

people,

of

The
 
New

affirms

for

of

world.
 
John
 
3:16
 
expresses
 
this
 
universal

clearly:
 “For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten 
Son,
 
that
 
whosoever
 
believeth
 
in

should
 
not
 
perish,
 
but
 
have
 
everlasting
 
life.”
 
(John
 
3:16,
 
KJV)
 This verse underscores the inclusive nature of the gospel, declaring that 
salvation
 
is
 
offered
 
universally.
 
God’s
 
love
 
is
 
not
 
confined
 
to
 
any
 
one
 
group
 
but
 
is
 
extended
 
to
 
“the
 
world,”
 
and
 
the
 
opportunity
 
for
 
eternal
 
life
 
is
 
open
 
to
 
whosoever
 
believes
 
in
 
Christ.
 
This
 
universal
 
offer
 
is
 
echoed
 
throughout
 
the
 
Scriptures,

seen
 
in
 
1
 
Timothy
 
2:4:
 
94

“Who will have all men to be saved, and to come unto the 
knowledge
 
of
 
the
 
truth.”
 
(1
 
Timothy
 
2:4,
 
KJV)
 John Stott, in his The Cross of Christ , writes that “the universal scope of 
God’s
 
love
 
is
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
gospel’s
 
invitation
 
to
 
all”
 
(Stott,
 
The
 
Cross
 
of
 
Christ
,
 
48).
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
God’s
 
will
 
that
 
any
 
should
 
perish
 
(2
 
Peter
 
3:9),
 
and
 
the
 
gospel
 
calls
 
all
 
men
 
everywhere
 
to
 
repent
 
(Acts
 
17:30).
 Yet, while salvation is offered universally, it is only applied particularly to 
those
 
who
 
respond
 
in
 
faith.
 
The
 
gospel
 
is
 
an
 
offer,
 
but
 
its
 
benefits
 
are
 
appropriated
 
by
 
individuals
 
through
 
personal
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ.
 
As
 
Jesus
 
Himself
 
says
 
in
 
Matthew
 
7:13-14:
 “Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad 
is
 
the
 
way,
 
that
 
leadeth
 
to
 
destruction,
 
and
 
many
 
there
 
be
 
which
 
go

thereat:
 
because

is

is

unto
 
life,
 
and
 
few
 
there
 
be
 
that

(Matthew
 
7:13-14,

 This suggests that while salvation is universally available, it is not

The
 
way
 
to

of

In

Lewis
 
in

Christianity
,
 
“The
 
Christian
 
doctrine
 
of

that

to

(Lewis,

,

Thus,

to

2. The Universal Offer vs. the Particular Application The tension between the universal offer of salvation and the particular 
application
 
of
 
salvation
 
is
 
often
 
a
 
point
 
of
 
theological
 
discussion.
 
God’s
 
offer
 
of
 
salvation
 
in
 
Christ
 
is
 
indeed
 
universal,
 
but
 
its
 
application
 
is
 
particular
 
in
 
that
 
it
 
only
 
becomes
 
effective
 
in
 
the
 
life
 
of
 
the
 
individual
 
who
 
believes
 
in

 The doctrine of the universal offer of salvation is sometimes called 
“unlimited
 
atonement,”
 
the
 
belief
 
that
 
Christ’s
 
death
 
was
 
for
 
all
 
people,
 
not
 
just
 
for
 
a
 
select
 
few.
 
This
 
view
 
is
 
grounded
 
in
 
verses
 
like
 
1
 
John
 
2:2:
 “And he is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, 
but
 
also
 
for
 
the
 
sins
 
of
 
the
 
whole
 
world.”
 
(1
 
John
 
2:2,
 
KJV)
 The apostle John emphasizes here that Christ’s redemption work is 
sufficient
 
for
 
the
 
entire
 
world,
 
and
 
God’s
 
desire
 
is
 
that
 
all
 
people
 
be
 
saved.
 
However,
 
this
 
does
 
not
 
imply
 
universalism,
 
the
 
belief
 
that
 
all
 
people
 
will
 
be
 
saved
 
regardless
 
of
 
their
 
response
 
to
 
the
 
gospel.
 
Rather,
 
it
 
means
 
that
 
Christ’s
 
death
 
has
 
made
 
salvation
 
available
 
to
 
all
 
who
 
will
 
believe.
 Yet, as John Calvin and other Reformed scholars have argued, the benefits 
of
 
Christ’s
 
death
 
are
 
applied
 
only
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
are
 
elected
 
by
 
God
 
and
 
come
 
to
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Calvin,
 
in
 
his
 
Institutes
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
Religion
,
 
explains
 
that
 
while
 
Christ’s
 
sacrifice
 
is
 
sufficient
 
for
 
all,
 
it
 
is
 
efficient
 
only
 
for
 
the
 
elect,
 
those
 
whom
 
God
 
has
 
chosen
 
for
 
salvation
 
(Calvin,
 
Institutes
,
 
3.24.15).
 
This
 
distinction
 
between
 
the
 
universal

and
 
the

application
 
of
 
salvation
 
is
 
crucial
 
to
 
understanding
 
the
 
scope
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan.

However, there are other scriptural passages that seem to present a

the

afresh,

 This passage is often cited by those who advocate for conditional security ,

 My Position: Assurance and the Believer’s Security In light of these theological considerations, I hold the position that a 
believer,

of
 
promise.”

 However, I also hold that apostasy remains a real and serious possibility. 
While

 The key to understanding this tension is that the believer’s perseverance in

J.I.

God’s
 
grace
 
preserves
 
the
 
believer,
 
ensuring

final
 
glorification. 4. The Perseverance of the Saints vs. Apostasy The doctrine of the perseverance of the saints teaches that those who are 
truly
 
saved
 
will
 
persevere
 
in
 
faith
 
to

is
 
the
 
believer’s

 The perseverance of the saints is not a passive, effortless continuation in

,

(Schreiner,
 
Romans
,

On the other hand, apostasy refers to the intentional abandonment of faith. 
Apostasy

real

the
 
goodness
 
of

but

Hebrews
 
10:38-39

But

 Conclusion: The scope and security of salvation are inseparable themes in the Christian 
faith.
 
Salvation
 
is
 
universally
 
offered,
 
and
 
it
 
is
 
God’s
 
will
 
that
 
all
 
come
 
to
 
repentance.
 
Yet,
 
salvation
 
is
 
particularly
 
applied
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
respond
 
in
 
faith,
 
and
 
its
 
security
 
rests
 
in
 
God’s
 
sovereign
 
grace.
 
While
 
Scripture
 
assures
 
believers
 
of
 
the
 
eternal
 
security
 
of
 
their
 
salvation,
 
it
 
also
 
warns
 
of
 
the
 
possibility
 
of
 
apostasy
 
and
 
urges
 
perseverance
 
in
 
the
 
faith.
 Ultimately, the believer’s assurance of salvation rests in the promises of 
God,
 
who
 
has
 
declared
 
that
 
nothing
 
can
 
separate
 
us
 
from
 
His
 
love.
 
As
 
we
 
live
 
out
 
our
 
salvation,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
remain
 
faithful,
 
knowing
 
that
 
God
 
will
 
preserve
 
His
 
own
 
to
 
the
 
end.
 
The
 
scope
 
of
 
salvation
 
is
 
vast,
 
but
 
its
 
security
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
the
 
unchanging
 
character

God,
 
who
 
is
 
faithful
 
to

the
 
good
 
work
 
He
 
has
 
begun
 
in
 
us.

Thus, the doctrine of salvation, with its expansive offer and secure

is
 
both
 
a
 
theological

the
 
glory
 
as

Glossary of Theological Terms Soteria Derived from the Greek word ’sōtēria’, it denotes salvation, deliverance, 
preservation,
 
and
 
safety.
 
In
 
Christian
 
theology,
 
Soteria
 
encapsulates
 
the
 
comprehensive
 
work
 
of
 
God
 
in
 
rescuing
 
humanity
 
from
 
the
 
bondage
 
of
 
sin
 
and
 
restoring
 
them
 
into
 
right
 
relationship
 
with
 
Himself.
 
It
 
involves
 
both
 
a
 
present
 
experience
 
of
 
grace
 
and
 
a
 
future
 
hope
 
of
 
eternal
 
glory.
 Salvation The divine initiative and process by which God delivers human beings 
from
 
sin,
 
death,
 
and
 
separation
 
from
 
Himself,
 
and
 
grants
 
them
 
eternal
 
life
 
through
 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Salvation
 
encompasses
 
justification,
 
sanctification,
 
and
 
glorification,
 
and
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
God’s
 
mercy,
 
grace,
 
and
 
eternal
 
purpose.
 Redemption The action by which God, in His mercy and grace, delivers humanity from 
the
 
consequences
 
of
 
sin.
 
While
 
sin
 
entered
 
the
 
world
 
through
 
human
 
choice,
 
God,
 
in
 
His
 
sovereignty,
 
was
 
able
 
and
 
willing
 
to
 
intervene
 
with
 
a
 
plan
 
of
 
salvation,
 
offering
 
forgiveness
 
and
 
restoration
 
through
 
the
 
sacrifice
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 Justification A forensic act of God in which He declares the sinner to be righteous on 
the
 
basis
 
of
 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Justification
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
process
 
but
 
a
 
once-for-all
 
legal
 
declaration,
 
grounded
 
in
 
the
 
imputed
 
righteousness
 
of
 
Christ.
 Sanctification 
163

The progressive work of the Holy Spirit in the life of the believer that 
makes
 
them
 
more
 
free
 
from
 
sin
 
and
 
more
 
like
 
Christ
 
in
 
their
 
actual
 
life.
 
It
 
is
 
both
 
positional,
 
having
 
been
 
set
 
apart
 
in
 
Christ,
 
and
 
experiential,
 
involving
 
a
 
lifelong
 
process
 
of
 
transformation.
 Glorification The final phase of the Christian salvation process wherein believers are 
given
 
resurrected,
 
imperishable
 
bodies
 
and
 
are
 
fully
 
conformed
 
to
 
the
 
image
 
of
 
Christ.
 
This
 
state
 
is
 
free
 
from
 
sin,
 
death,
 
and
 
suffering,
 
and
 
it
 
consummates
 
eternal
 
life
 
with
 
God.
 Eternal Life The unending life with God that believers receive through faith in Jesus 
Christ.
 
Eternal
 
life
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
future
 
promise
 
but
 
a
 
present
 
reality
 
that
 
begins
 
at
 
the
 
moment
 
of
 
salvation,
 
as
 
believers
 
enter
 
into
 
fellowship
 
with
 
God,
 
experiencing
 
His
 
love,
 
peace,
 
and
 
joy.
 The Fall The event in which Adam and Eve, through their disobedience to God, 
introduced
 
sin
 
into
 
the
 
world.
 
While
 
God
 
allowed
 
this
 
to
 
happen
 
according
 
to
 
His
 
sovereign
 
will
,
 
He
 
did
 
not
 
actively
 
cause
 
it.
 
The
 
fall
 
was
 
part
 
of
 
God’s
 
plan
 
in
 
that
 
He,
 
in
 
His
 
foreknowledge,
 
knew
 
it
 
would
 
occur,
 
but
 
He
 
also
 
had
 
a
 
provision
 
in
 
place
 
to
 
redeem
 
humanity
 
through
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 Atonement In the Old Testament, atonement refers to the covering or cleansing of sin 
through
 
sacrificial
 
rituals,
 
temporarily
 
restoring
 
fellowship
 
between
 
God
 
and
 
His
 
people.
 
In
 
the
 
New
 
Testament,
 
atonement
 
signifies
 
the
 
complete
 
164

removal of sin through the death of Jesus Christ, fully reconciling 
humanity
 
to
 
God.
 Infralapsarianism This view teaches that God’s decree to save came after His decree to allow 
the
 
Fall.
 
 
It
 
affirms
 
that:
 ● God decreed to create humanity. ● God permitted the Fall (but did not cause it). ● God then decreed to save human. ● God provided salvation through Christ. Supralapsarianism: A theological view regarding the order of God’s decrees, asserting that 
God’s
 
decision
 
to
 
save
 
or
 
condemn
 
was
 
made
 
before
 
the
 
decree
 
to
 
allow
 
the
 
fall
 
of
 
humanity.
 
In
 
this
 
view,
 
the
 
fall
 
of
 
man
 
is
 
seen
 
as
 
part
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
plan
 
to
 
display
 
both
 
His
 
justice
 
and
 
mercy.
 
Supralapsarianism
 
emphasizes
 
that
 
the
 
plan
 
of
 
salvation
 
was
 
not
 
a
 
reaction
 
to
 
the
 
fall
 
but
 
part
 
of
 
God’s
 
deliberate
 
and
 
preordained
 
plan.
 Theological Anthropology: 
The
 
study
 
of
 
humanity
 
from
 
a
 
biblical
 
perspective,
 
focusing
 
on
 
the
 
nature
 
of
 
mankind,
 
including
 
the
 
fall,
 
sin,
 
and
 
the
 
restoration
 
of
 
humanity
 
through
 
Christ.
 
This
 
field
 
addresses
 
the
 
relationship
 
between
 
humanity
 
and
 
God,
 
highlighting
 
both
 
human
 
dignity
 
and
 
human
 
depravity.
 The Kingdom of God: 
The
 
reign
 
of
 
God
 
over
 
His
 
creation,
 
both
 
now
 
and
 
in
 
the
 
future.
 
The
 
Kingdom
 
of
 
God
 
is
 
both
 
a
 
present
 
reality,
 
as
 
believers
 
experience
 
God’s
 
165

rule in their lives, and a future hope, as God’s kingdom will be fully 
established
 
at
 
Christ’s
 
return.
 Grace: 
The
 
unmerited
 
favor
 
of
 
God,
 
extended
 
to
 
humanity
 
in
 
the
 
form
 
of
 
forgiveness,
 
salvation,
 
and
 
spiritual
 
empowerment.
 
Grace
 
is
 
foundational
 
to
 
the
 
Christian
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation,
 
as
 
it
 
is
 
by
 
grace
 
that
 
people
 
are
 
saved,
 
not
 
by
 
their
 
works
 
(Ephesians
 
2:8-9).
 Mercy: 
God’s
 
loving-kindness
 
toward
 
humanity,
 
particularly
 
in
 
withholding
 
judgment
 
and
 
offering
 
forgiveness.
 
While
 
grace
 
gives
 
us
 
what
 
we
 
do
 
not
 
deserve
 
(salvation),
 
mercy
 
withholds
 
from
 
us
 
what
 
we
 
do
 
deserve
 
(eternal
 
separation
 
from
 
God).
 Sovereignty of God: 
The
 
belief
 
that
 
God
 
has
 
ultimate
 
authority
 
over
 
all
 
things,
 
not
 
necessarily
 
by
 
controlling
 
every
 
action,
 
but
 
by
 
being
 
fully
 
aware
 
of
 
all
 
events
 
and
 
having
 
the
 
power
 
and
 
will
 
to
 
intervene
 
as
 
He
 
chooses.
 
In
 
the
 
context
 
of
 
the
 
fall
 
of
 
man,
 
God’s
 
sovereignty
 
means
 
that
 
while
 
He
 
was
 
not
 
the
 
direct
 
cause
 
of
 
the
 
fall,
 
He
 
knew
 
it
 
would
 
occur
 
and,
 
in
 
His
 
sovereign
 
wisdom,
 
already
 
had
 
a
 
plan
 
of
 
redemption
 
in
 
place.
 
God’s
 
sovereignty
 
ensures
 
that
 
despite
 
the
 
presence
 
of
 
sin,
 
He
 
is
 
able
 
and
 
more
 
than
 
able
 
to
 
deliver
 
humanity
 
from
 
the
 
fall
 
and
 
its
 
consequences.
 Foreknowledge of God: 
 
God’s
 
perfect
 
and
 
eternal
 
knowledge
 
of
 
all
 
things
 
before
 
they
 
happen.
 
This
 
includes
 
His
 
knowing
 
that
 
humanity
 
would
 
fall
 
into
 
sin.
 
While
 
God
 
did
 
not
 
directly
 
cause
 
the
 
fall,
 
He
 
knew
 
it
 
would
 
happen
 
and
 
prepared
 
a
 
166

redemptive plan long before creation. His foreknowledge assures us that 
nothing
 
in
 
history
 
is
 
beyond
 
His
 
understanding
 
and
 
control.
 
167
